title: US Holds Off Blacklisting DeepSeek as G7 Grapples With AI Model Access
subhead: The Trump administration delays adding DeepSeek and 100+ Chinese firms to the Entity List, G7 leaders debate trusted partner access to advanced US AI models, and Anthropic suspends its most powerful models for all users after a US export order — all while a US-Iran deal promises to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

sections.heading: US Delays Blacklisting DeepSeek and 100+ Chinese Firms
sections.body:
The US Commerce Department has held off adding DeepSeek, memory chipmaker CXMT, and more than 100 other Chinese companies flagged as national security risks to its Entity List, despite an interagency committee approving them for addition last year. Reuters first reported the scale of the backlog — at least 75 Chinese entities in advanced semiconductor production, semiconductor equipment, and AI modeling had been approved for listing but never published.

Since late 2025, Jeffrey Kessler, under secretary of commerce for industry and security, has sought to avoid listing Chinese parties for fear of escalating US-China tensions, according to sources familiar with the matter. The Entity List has seen no new additions since October, the longest gap in over a decade. Philip Luck of the Center for Strategic and International Studies noted that the lack of listings 'is likely allowing American technology to reach adversaries.' Former Commerce Department official Kevin Kurland said the freeze shows 'trade policy is overshadowing the use of a critical national security tool.'

Among those stalled: Chinese companies that supplied Russian drones recovered in Poland, firms selling restricted Nvidia chips to Chinese universities, and drone and robot dog makers for China's military. DeepSeek was flagged for supporting China's military and intelligence operations and using Southeast Asian shell companies to access advanced US chips. Anthropic and OpenAI both reported that DeepSeek targeted their models for capability extraction.

China's foreign ministry spokesperson Lin Jian said the US should cease 'politicizing, instrumentalizing, and weaponizing' economic and tech issues.
sections.whyItMatters: The Entity List freeze reveals a fundamental tension in US policy: trade negotiation priorities are overriding export control enforcement, creating a gap where approved national security restrictions sit unpublished for months.

sections.heading: G7 Debates Trusted Partner Access to Advanced AI Models
sections.body:
G7 leaders discussed granting 'trusted partners' access to advanced US AI models at the summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, according to Reuters. The discussions, led primarily by US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick on the sidelines of the opening dinner on Monday, could allow G7 countries to use models like Anthropic's Mythos for cybersecurity defense against rivals such as China.

The debate comes days after the US government ordered Anthropic to block all foreign national access to its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models on national security grounds. Anthropic complied by suspending access for all customers, not just foreign nationals, saying it found only 'minor vulnerabilities' in its own review. Anthropic stated that if the government's standard were applied across platforms, 'it would essentially halt all new model deployments for all frontier model providers.'

Before the ban, Anthropic had given Mythos access to organizations in more than 15 countries for vulnerability scanning. Cybersecurity experts believe Mythos, designed to find flaws in computer code, could also turbocharge attacks on banking systems. India is among countries seeking access to study the model's implications. A Trump White House official said the president's team has 'an open line of communication with our allies' on the national security concerns.

AI executives from Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google are expected at a G7 working lunch on Wednesday to discuss regulation, AI infrastructure, and networks.
sections.whyItMatters: The US is simultaneously restricting and selectively expanding access to frontier AI models — a dual-track approach that will define how AI capability spreads among allies versus adversaries. The outcome shapes which countries get defensive AI tools and which are locked out.

sections.heading: US Invokes Defense Production Act as Iran War Strains Weapons Stockpiles
sections.body:
President Trump invoked the Defense Production Act on Tuesday to address 'systemic constraints in the munitions industrial base,' according to a presidential memo. The order delegates authority to the Defense Secretary to use the Cold War-era law to accelerate production of munitions, missiles, and critical equipment.

The move comes as a CSIS analysis found the US may have expended more than half its inventory of four critical munitions categories, including Tomahawk missiles, during the Iran campaign. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told CBS that US stockpiles are 'great' and 'only getting stronger,' though he acknowledged in April testimony that replenishment could take 'months to years.'

The memo invokes a section of the 1950 law allowing voluntary agreements between government and private firms. Michael Cadenazzi, assistant secretary of defense for industrial base policy, said the Pentagon has been working since September to launch such an agreement. Republican Sen. John Cornyn of Texas said the Pentagon is 'running short of funding' for weapons acquisition. The administration is seeking a record $1.5 trillion defense budget through reconciliation.
sections.whyItMatters: The Defense Production Act invocation signals that the Iran conflict has exposed real weaknesses in the US munitions supply chain — weaknesses that also have implications for any potential Pacific contingency, where long-range missiles would be needed in far greater quantities.

sections.heading: US Plans Permanent Marine Corps Weapons Stockpile in Australia
sections.body:
The US Marine Corps is planning a permanent, war-ready weapons stockpile in southeastern Australia, beyond the range of most Chinese missiles, according to US Navy tender documents and officials. The $30 million facility will be built at the Australian military base at Bandiana in rural Victoria, with warehouses and offices for 'critical forward provisioning.'

The stockpile — a first for the Marine Corps in Australia — will reach full capacity by 2028. Equipment will initially be stored in Melbourne before relocation. The Navy is engaging a global defense contractor to employ around 110 engineers, mechanics, and specialists to manage the facility. A first Asia-Pacific land stockpile is expected to open this year in the Philippines, near South China Sea flashpoints.

The Lowy Institute's Sam Roggeveen said China's ability to strike northern Australia with ballistic missiles was 'likely a relevant consideration' in siting the stockpile in Victoria, but warned that 'once these facilities are operational, they would be obvious targets for China.' ANU professor John Blaxland said Australia's location is increasingly significant given concerns about the vulnerability of the US base on Guam. The Pentagon has requested $500 million next year to improve prepositioning across the Indo-Pacific.
sections.whyItMatters: The Bandiana stockpile deepens the integration of Australian territory into US force posture in the Pacific, placing war reserves beyond China's missile reach — but also making Australia a more visible target in any great-power confrontation.

sections.heading: SoftBank and OpenAI Launch AI Cybersecurity Service for Japan
sections.body:
SoftBank Group launched an AI-powered cybersecurity service for Japanese enterprises on Tuesday, built on OpenAI technology through their 50:50 joint venture SB OAI Japan. CEO Masayoshi Son called Japan's vulnerability to cyberattacks 'a crisis,' comparing the threat to 'an assault by machine guns instead of rifle shots.' The service will target Japan's top 3,000 companies running critical infrastructure — airports, power systems, and transportation.

The 'Patching as a Service' offering diagnoses vulnerabilities and generates remediation plans using OpenAI's large language models. Son framed the launch as a duty: 'I feel it is our duty.' OpenAI CEO Sam Altman was scheduled to attend but appeared only via video, citing the early arrival of his daughter. OpenAI chief researcher Mark Chen attended in his place.

The timing is notable: the launch coincides with the US government's order blocking foreign access to Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models — products that were positioned as cybersecurity tools. Nikkei Asia highlighted this directly in its headline, noting SoftBank is launching OpenAI cybersecurity in Japan 'as US restricts rival model.' Japan is effectively choosing OpenAI as its sovereign AI cybersecurity partner while Anthropic's competing offering is pulled from foreign access.
sections.whyItMatters: As Washington restricts Anthropic's models from foreign hands, OpenAI is filling the gap with a sovereign deployment for Japan — turning US export control policy into a competitive advantage for one American AI company over another.

sections.heading: Iran Deal to Reopen Strait of Hormuz; BOJ Hikes Rates to 1%
sections.body:
A tentative US-Iran deal, due to be signed Friday in Switzerland, would immediately reopen the Strait of Hormuz and allow Iran to sell oil without restrictions. Iran would receive at least $300 billion for reconstruction, and the US would work to end all American and UN sanctions if a final nuclear agreement is reached within 60 days. The deal also includes a ceasefire in Lebanon between Israel and Hezbollah.

Trump acknowledged uncertainty about the signing, saying 'You never know with deals,' and added: 'If I don't like it, we'll go back to shooting at them, dropping bombs.' The deal falls short of Trump's stated war goals of ending Iran's nuclear and missile programs.

Even with the strait reopening, analysts warn it will take months for oil flows to normalize. Neil Shearing of Capital Economics estimated roughly 80% of energy flows will resume by end of Q3. Oil stocks have been falling at 5.3 million barrels per day between March and May, according to the US EIA, with inventories reaching critical levels. Brent crude peaked around $120/barrel during the conflict.

Meanwhile, the Bank of Japan raised its benchmark rate to 1% on Tuesday — the highest since 1995 — citing a weak yen and inflationary pressures from the war-driven oil shock. Japan imports nearly all its oil and gas. Governor Kazuo Ueda was hospitalized and did not attend the meeting; Deputy Governor Shinichi Uchida said the BOJ will continue raising rates. The Nikkei 225 briefly topped 70,000 before the announcement.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz deal removes the most acute energy supply threat of 2026, but the gap between a signed memorandum and restored oil flows will keep pressure on importing economies — especially Japan, which just raised rates to a 31-year high partly because of the energy shock.

sections.heading: UK Hits Russian Shadow Fleet With Largest Sanctions Package; Singapore AI Exports Hit 22-Year High
sections.body:
The UK imposed 70 new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow fleet, military procurement supply chains, and illicit finance networks, timed with the G7 summit. The package targets 30 shadow fleet vessels — half of which transported over $4.3 billion worth of oil and oil products in the past year alone. Two Russian insurers, ALFASTRAKHOVANIE and VSK, were also sanctioned. The UK has now sanctioned 73 oil tankers, more than any other nation, compared to 39 by the US and 19 by the EU. Foreign Secretary David Lammy pushed G7 partners to maintain pressure on Russia's war funding at the summit in Italy.

Separately, Singapore reported that non-oil domestic exports surged 38.4% year-on-year in May — the sharpest growth in over two decades. Electronics exports rose 94.8%, with integrated circuits up 80.9% and disk media products up 227.8%. Exports to Taiwan grew 135.2%, to the US 80.9%, and to China 31%. Analysts at OCBC and eToro traced the surge directly to hyperscaler capital expenditure: Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, and Amazon collectively lifted 2026 capex plans to over $700 billion. Singapore Semiconductor Industry Association executive director Ang Wee Seng said a significant portion of the electronics uplift is 'directly or indirectly connected to AI demand.' Canada also entered the picture at the G7: a hot mic caught PM Mark Carney telling Trump that Canada's deal allowing 49,000 Chinese EVs annually at a 6.1% tariff (vs. the standard 100%) represents 'less than three per cent of our market' — Trump replied, 'That's good. I like that.'
sections.whyItMatters: The UK shadow fleet sanctions and Singapore's AI-driven export surge are two sides of the same coin: the infrastructure of global trade is being reshaped — by force in the case of Russian oil, by demand in the case of AI chips. Canada's EV concession to China, meanwhile, shows how even close US allies are carving out limited space for Chinese technology despite Washington's push for decoupling.

bottomLine: June 17 marked a day of deliberate contradictions in US tech-power policy: the same administration that blocked foreign access to Anthropic's frontier models is discussing selective G7 access to those very models, while simultaneously holding off on blacklisting DeepSeek and 100+ Chinese firms that interagency review has already approved. SoftBank's OpenAI cybersecurity launch in Japan shows how export restrictions on one company become market opportunity for another. Meanwhile, the Defense Production Act invocation and the Australia stockpile decision confirm that the hardware side of great-power competition — munitions, missiles, prepositioned gear — is accelerating regardless of what happens to software controls. The Iran deal reopening Hormuz takes the sharpest edge off the energy crisis, but the BOJ's rate hike to a 31-year high shows the economic damage has already been absorbed.
sources: Reuters, CNBC, Economic Times, AP News, SCMP, SBS, DW, India Today, CBC, Channel News Asia, Nikkei Asia, CBS News, UK Gov, CSIS, Straits Times, Business Times Singapore, Lowy Institute, Capital Economics, Vortexa, eToro, Singapore Semiconductor Industry Association
